techniques.evidence_quote: This access token was issued to a third party contractor during the testing of the website 2050.earth
a legitimate Amazon Simple Email Service (SES) token issued to a third-party contractor was recently used by threat actors
a legitimate Amazon Simple Email Service (SES) token issued to a third-party contractor was recently used by threat actors
lure users to fake websites collecting credentials for Microsoft online services
The phishing e-mails are usually arriving in the form of 'Fax notifications' and lure users to fake websites collecting credentials for Microsoft online services

they used an official Kaspersky email and sent the emails from Amazon Web Services infrastructure, which likely helped them reach their targets mailboxes by easily evading most Secure Email Gateway (SEGs) protections
Kaspersky security experts linked the phishing attempts to multiple cybercriminals who used two phishing kits in this campaign, one known as Iamtheboss and another named MIRCBOOT

they used an official Kaspersky email and sent the emails from Amazon Web Services infrastructure, which likely helped them reach their targets mailboxes by easily evading most Secure Email Gateway (SEGs) protections

attack_chain.technique_name: Cloud Instance Metadata API / Unsecured Credentials: Cloud Instance Metadata API
attack_chain.description: Attackers obtained a legitimate Amazon SES API token that had been issued to a third-party contractor working on Kaspersky's 2050.earth website. The exact theft mechanism is unspecified, but the token was exposed/stolen from the contractor.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: a legitimate Amazon Simple Email Service (SES) token issued to a third-party contractor was recently used by threat actors

attack_chain.description: Attackers deployed two phishing kits (Iamtheboss and MIRCBOOT) to set up credential-harvesting landing pages impersonating Microsoft Office 365 login pages.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: Kaspersky security experts linked the phishing attempts to multiple cybercriminals who used two phishing kits in this campaign, one known as Iamtheboss and another named MIRCBOOT

attack_chain.description: Attackers leveraged the stolen SES token and Kaspersky's official email domain (noreply@sm.kaspersky.com) to send phishing emails from trusted AWS infrastructure, bypassing Secure Email Gateway protections.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: they used an official Kaspersky email and sent the emails from Amazon Web Services infrastructure, which likely helped them reach their targets mailboxes by easily evading most Secure Email Gateway (SEGs) protections

attack_chain.description: Phishing emails disguised as 'Fax notifications' were sent to Office 365 users, containing links redirecting victims to fake Microsoft credential-harvesting pages.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: The phishing e-mails are usually arriving in the form of 'Fax notifications' and lure users to fake websites collecting credentials for Microsoft online services

attack_chain.description: Victims who clicked the phishing link were directed to fake Microsoft Office 365 / OWA login pages designed to harvest their credentials.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: lure users to fake websites collecting credentials for Microsoft online services

initial_access_vector: Stolen Amazon SES API token obtained from a third-party contractor who had been issued the token for testing Kaspersky's 2050.earth website
persistence_mechanism: None identified — the SES token served as persistent sending capability until revoked by Kaspersky upon discovery
privilege_escalation: Not applicable — attackers abused an existing legitimate token; no escalation observed
